title: Yellow Skin on Your Cat's Ears? Understanding Feline Jaundice
meta_desc: If your cat has yellow skin or ears, it could be jaundice. Learn why this is a high-urgency medical emergency and what steps you should take now.
content_html:
What is Icterus (Jaundice)?

Finding a yellow tint on your cat's ears, gums, or the whites of their eyes is a clinical condition known as jaundice or icterus. This discoloration is caused by an accumulation of bilirubin, a yellow pigment formed by the breakdown of red blood cells. Under normal conditions, the liver processes and excretes this pigment; however, when the liver is overwhelmed or diseased, or when red blood cells are being destroyed too quickly, the pigment builds up in the tissues.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, yellowing of the skin is always considered a medical emergency in cats. It is a visible sign of a significant internal health crisis, such as liver disease, feline infectious peritonitis (FIP), toxins, or severe anemia. Because cats can deteriorate rapidly when their liver function is compromised, you should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately for an evaluation.

How a Photo Helps Triage

When you contact your veterinarian, providing a clear, high-resolution photo of the yellowing (especially on the ears or gums) can be incredibly helpful. Lighting can sometimes play tricks on the eyes, and a photo allows the triage team to confirm the severity of the icterus remotely. This helps them prioritize your cat's intake and prepare the necessary diagnostic equipment, such as blood work and ultrasound, before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: Can I wait until the morning to see a vet?
faqs.answer: No. Jaundice is a sign of systemic failure or serious disease. Waiting even a few hours can significantly worsen the prognosis for your cat.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms to look for?
faqs.answer: Common concurrent symptoms include lethargy, loss of appetite, vomiting, hiding, or changes in urine color (often appearing dark orange or brown).

faqs.question: Is jaundice in cats curable?
faqs.answer: The prognosis depends entirely on the underlying cause. Some conditions, like certain infections or fatty liver disease (hepatic lipidosis), can be treated successfully if caught early.
